note_data.title: Electricity
keyPoints: Electric current is the flow of electric charges through a conductor — measured in Amperes
Ohm's Law states that current through a conductor is directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance
Resistance is the opposition to the flow of electric current — measured in Ohms (Ω)
Power is the rate of doing work or energy consumed per unit time — measured in Watts
Electric energy is the work done by electric current — measured in Joules or kWh
Series circuits have single path for current flow, parallel circuits have multiple paths
In series connection, current remains same but voltage divides
In parallel connection, voltage remains same but current divides
WAPDA generates electricity at Tarbela Dam and supplies to cities like Karachi through transmission lines

definitions.definition: Electric current is the amount of charge flowing through any cross-section of a conductor in unit time. I = Q/t
The potential difference between two points is the work done in moving unit positive charge from one point to another. V = W/Q
Resistance is the property of a conductor which opposes the flow of current through it. R = V/I
Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is consumed or converted into other forms of energy. P = VI
EMF is the energy per unit charge supplied by a source like battery or generator. It is measured in volts.

importantQuestions.question: Define Ohm's Law and write its mathematical form.
importantQuestions.answer: Ohm's Law states that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage across it, provided temperature remains constant. Mathematically: V = IR or R = V/I

importantQuestions.question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance when three resistors are connected in series.
importantQuestions.answer: When resistors R₁, R₂, R₃ are connected in series: Current through each resistor is same (I). Total voltage V = V₁ + V₂ + V₃. By Ohm's law: V₁ = IR₁, V₂ = IR₂, V₃ = IR₃. Therefore: V = IR₁ + IR₂ + IR₃ = I(R₁ + R₂ + R₃). If Rs is equivalent resistance, then V = IRs. Hence: IRs = I(R₁ + R₂ + R₃). Therefore: Rs = R₁ + R₂ + R₃

mcqs.question: The SI unit of electric current is:

options.A: Current is same through each resistor
options.B: Voltage is different across each resistor
options.C: Voltage is same across each resistor

mcqs.question: The power dissipated in a resistor is given by:

commonMistakes: Students confuse series and parallel connections — remember series has one path, parallel has multiple paths
Always write the formula before solving numerical problems
Students forget to convert units (mA to A, kΩ to Ω) before calculations
In parallel resistance formula, students forget to take reciprocal at the end
Students mix up current and voltage behavior in series vs parallel circuits
examTips: This chapter usually has 2-3 MCQs and appears in every BSEK paper
Circuit diagram questions are very common — practice drawing series and parallel circuits
Ohm's law derivation and numerical problems appear frequently
Memorize all power formulas: P = VI, P = I²R, P = V²/R
Series and parallel resistance derivations are high-weightage topics
note_data.keyDiagram: Circuit diagrams showing series and parallel connections with resistors, battery, and ammeter/voltmeter positions. Students must show conventional current direction and proper circuit symbols as per STBB standards.
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q4 (5 marks) — asked students to define electric current, potential difference and resistance with their SI units
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q6 (8 marks) — required explanation of Ohm's law with circuit diagram and numerical problem on V=IR
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — tested definitions of electric power and energy with their units and relationship
BSEK 2020 SSC-II Q5 (6 marks) — asked to differentiate between series and parallel circuits with advantages and disadvantages

workedExample.question: Define electric current and calculate the current flowing through a resistor of 10Ω when 5V potential difference is applied across it. (5 marks)
steps: Write textbook definition of electric current
State Ohm's law formula V = IR
Rearrange formula to find I = V/R
workedExample.answer: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor. It is measured in amperes (A). Using Ohm's law: V = IR, where I = V/R. Given: V = 5V, R = 10Ω. Therefore: I = 5/10 = 0.5A
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for correct answer with unit

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for definitions of current, voltage, and resistance.
Many students confuse electric power (P = VI) with electric energy (E = Pt). Remember power is rate of doing work while energy is total work done.
In circuit problems, always show the formula first, then substitute values, and include proper SI units in the final answer to secure full marks.
note_data.quickRecap: Electric current is rate of flow of charge (I = Q/t). Potential difference is work done per unit charge (V = W/Q). Ohm's law states V = IR for constant temperature. Electric power P = VI and electric energy E = Pt. Series circuits have same current, parallel circuits have same voltage.
